sections.label: Methods
sections.retrieval_query: study design randomization blinding eligibility primary endpoint statistical methods
sections.drafting_instructions: Use subheadings: Trial Design and Oversight, Participants, Randomization and Blinding, Interventions, Outcomes, Statistical Analysis. State IRB approval and registration number (NCT#). Copy eligibility criteria verbatim from protocol. Power calculation required in Statistical Analysis. Past tense throughout. Word limit: 1200.

sections.label: Results
sections.retrieval_query: primary endpoint results p-value confidence interval adverse events
sections.drafting_instructions: Use subheadings: Participants, Primary Outcome, Secondary Outcomes, Adverse Events. Lead with CONSORT-style participant flow. Primary outcome: absolute difference or ratio, 95% CI, p-value. Prespecified subgroups in a separate paragraph. Safety: all Grade ≥3 AEs by group, serious AEs, discontinuations. All numbers must be citable. Past tense. Word limit: 1200.

sections.label: Discussion
sections.retrieval_query: clinical significance prior studies limitations generalizability future directions
sections.drafting_instructions: NEJM discussions are more expansive than JAMA. 5–6 paragraphs: (1) primary finding restated with effect size; (2) comparison to prior trials with citations; (3) mechanistic explanation; (4) subgroup findings if applicable; (5) explicit limitations — be candid; (6) clinical implications and future work. Present tense for established facts; past tense for this trial. Word limit: 1200.

sections.label: Introduction
sections.retrieval_query: disease burden unmet need study rationale prior evidence
sections.drafting_instructions: 3–4 paragraphs. NEJM expects broader framing than JAMA. Open with the clinical problem at population scale. Paragraph 2: prior evidence and where it falls short. Paragraph 3: mechanistic or conceptual basis for this intervention. Final paragraph: state the hypothesis and primary endpoint precisely. Present tense for established facts; past for prior studies. Word limit: 650.

sections.label: Conclusion
sections.drafting_instructions: 2–4 sentences. Restate the key finding (effect size, direction), identify who benefits, and state the clinical or policy implication. NEJM allows slightly more interpretive language than JAMA but no overclaiming. No new data. No references. Word limit: 200.

sections.label: Abstract
sections.retrieval_query: study design primary endpoint results conclusions significance
presets: Summarize per NEJM structured abstract format
sections.drafting_instructions: Structured abstract with these headings: Background, Methods, Results, Conclusions. Keep each section to 1–3 sentences. Results: lead with primary endpoint (point estimate, 95% CI, p-value). Include NCT registration number in Methods. Do not include data not in the article. Past tense. Word limit: 250.

sections.label: Plain Language Summary
sections.drafting_instructions: Written for patients and caregivers. US Grade 8–10 reading level. Define all medical terms on first use. Lead with one sentence summarizing the key finding in plain language. Structure: What was the problem? What did researchers do? What did they find? What does it mean for patients? Close with 3–5 Key Takeaways bullets. No citations. No jargon.

sections.label: Citations
sections.drafting_instructions: Annotate every factual claim, statistic, or assertion with a citation marker ([N], [N?], [?], or [!]). Return the annotated draft followed by a numbered Vancouver-style reference list and a summary of all unresolved markers.
